covid_19.explain_yes_answers_here_covid_19:
Ethan tested positive for COVID-19 in mid-November 2020 after a known exposure on his club soccer team. One of his teammates’ older siblings tested positive, and several players, including Ethan, developed mild symptoms a few days later. Ethan’s main symptoms were low-grade fever (around 100.5°F), a dry cough, fatigue, and temporary loss of taste and smell. His symptoms started on 2020-11-16, and his PCR test came back positive on 2020-11-18.

He was never hospitalized. We managed his illness at home with guidance from his pediatrician, Dr. Melissa Chan at Northside Pediatrics in Columbus, Ohio. We monitored his temperature and oxygen saturation (we bought a fingertip pulse oximeter); his oxygen levels stayed between 97–99% the entire time. He did not have any chest pain, difficulty breathing at rest, or signs of dehydration. The fever resolved in about 4 days, the cough improved within a week, and his sense of taste and smell returned gradually over the next 2–3 weeks.

He was not diagnosed with MIS-C and did not show any concerning inflammatory symptoms such as persistent high fever, abdominal pain, rash, or red eyes. Because his case was mild and he did not have any history of heart problems, his pediatrician did not feel that heart or lung imaging or referral to a cardiologist was necessary. She did, however, recommend that he rest completely from sports for 10 days after symptom onset and then ease back into activity over the following 1–2 weeks, paying attention to any chest pain, unusual shortness of breath, or palpitations. None of those concerning symptoms occurred.

By early December 2020, Ethan had fully returned to his normal energy level and regular soccer practice schedule. He has been able to participate in both school and club soccer without any lingering respiratory issues, chest discomfort, or fatigue. As of the 2021–22 school year, he is back to full participation in all sports and physical education activities with no restrictions.

Regarding testing, he had a PCR test on 2020-11-18 that confirmed the infection. He also had a negative PCR test on 2020-12-02 in order to return to in-person training with his club team, per their protocol at that time.

He received the Pfizer-BioNTech COVID-19 vaccine as soon as it became available to his age group. He got his first dose on 2021-05-18 and his second dose on 2021-06-08 at the Franklin County Community Vaccination Clinic. After the first dose, he experienced mild arm soreness and a slight headache that resolved within 24 hours. After the second dose, he had more noticeable fatigue and chills that evening and a low-grade fever (99–100°F) the next day, but these side effects went away completely within about 36 hours. He has not had any ongoing issues or adverse reactions related to the vaccine.

In the past 3 months, he has not had any direct or known exposure to someone with a confirmed COVID-19 infection, to our knowledge. His school has had strict masking and distancing protocols, and we have been notified any time there was a positive case in his grade; in each of those instances, he was not identified as a close contact and did not require quarantine. He continues to follow recommended precautions, including masking indoors when required and frequent handwashing.
